coredata.dc:title: Investigation of interaction effects on dual-frequency driven cavitation dynamics in a two-bubble system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Dui
Yang, Qianru
Lei, Shuang
Fu, Jia
Ji, Xiaojuan
Wang, Xiuxin
coredata.dc:description: The cavitation dynamics of a two-bubble system in viscoelastic media excited by dual-frequency ultrasound is studied numerically with a focus on the effects of inter-bubble interactions. Compared to the isolated bubble cases, the enhancement or suppression effects can be exerted on the amplitude and nonlinearity of the bubble oscillations to different degrees. Moreover, the interaction effects are found to be highly sensitive to multiple paramount parameters related to the two-bubble system, the dual-frequency ultrasound and the medium viscoelasticity. Specifically, the larger bubble of a two-bubble system shows a stronger effect on the smaller one, and this effect becomes more pronounced when the larger bubble undergoes harmonic and/or subharmonic resonances as well as the two bubbles get closer (e.g., d 0 < 100 μm). For the influences of the dual-frequency excitation, the results show that the bubbles can achieve enhanced harmonic and/or subharmonic oscillations as the frequency combinations with small frequency differences (e.g., Δf < 0.2 MHz) close to the corresponding resonance frequencies of bubbles, and the interaction effects are consequently intensified. Similarly, the bubble oscillations and the interaction effects can also be enhanced as the acoustic pressure amplitude of each frequency component is equal and the pressure amplitude pA increases. Above a pressure threshold (pA = 215 kPa), a larger bubble undergoes period 2 (P2) oscillations, which can force a smaller bubble to change its oscillation pattern from period 1 (P1) into P2 oscillations. In addition, it is found that the medium viscosity dampens the bubble oscillations while the medium elasticity affects the bubble resonances, accordingly exhibiting stronger interaction effects at smaller viscosities (e.g., μ < 4 mPa·s) or certain elasticities (approximately G = 70–120 kPa, G = 160–200 kPa and G = 640–780 kPa) at which the bubble resonances occur. The study can contribute to a better understanding of the complex dynamic behaviors of interacting cavitation bubbles in viscoelastic tissues for high efficient cavitation-mediated biomedical applications using dual-frequency ultrasound.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acoustic cavitation
Dual-frequency excitation
Bubble dynamics
Inter-bubble interactions
Viscoelasticity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modelling two bubble dynamics under dual-frequency driving
Modelling viscoelastic effects of surrounding media on bubble dynamics
Bifurcation analysis method with Poincaré section of dual-frequency driving
Interaction effects of two bubbles under dual-frequency driving
Representative interaction effects on cavitation dynamics
Effects of the parameters related to the two-bubble system
Effects of acoustic settings with dual-frequency excitation
Combinations of the two component frequencies
Ultrasound amplitude ratio between the two components

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Normalized bubble radius (R 1/R 01) of a small bubble (R 01 = 2.5 μm) versus the period number of dual-frequency driving (n) in some representative cases. (a) Single bubble case and (b-e) two interacting bubble cases in which the second bubbles have different initial radii, i.e., (b) R 02 = 3.2 μm, (c) R 02 = 7 μm, (d) R 02 = 8.2 μm and (e) R 02 = 10 μm.

Normalized bubble radius (R 1/R 01) of a large bubble (R 01 = 8.2 μm) versus the period number of dual-frequency driving (n) in some representative cases. (a) Single bubble case and (b-d) two interacting bubble cases where the second bubbles have different initial radii, i.e., (b) R 02 = 3.2 μm, (c) R 02 = 7 μm and (d) R 02 = 10 μm.

(a) Oscillation amplitudes (R max/R 0) and (b, c) bifurcation diagrams of a two-bubble system with R 01 = 2.5 μm as a function of the initial radius of the second bubble (R 02 = 1–10 μm). (d-f) The corresponding results in the case of R 01 = 8.2 μm. The symbols ‘w/o’ and ‘w’ mean the cases without and with the interaction effects between bubbles, respectively.

Oscillation amplitudes of a two-bubble system, in which (a) one bubble with an initial radius of R 01 = 2.5 μm and (b) the other bubble with a varying initial radius (R 02 = 1–10 μm), as a function of R 02 at various initial inter-bubble distances (d 0). (c) Oscillation amplitudes and (d) bifurcation diagrams of a two-bubble system (R 01 = 2.5 μm and R 02 = 8.2 μm) as a function of d 0.

Oscillation amplitudes of a two-bubble system (R 01 = 3.2 μm and R 02 = 8.2 μm) as a function of the frequency f 0 = (f 1 + f 2)/2. A change of the f 0 can obtain different frequency combinations of the two components of dual-frequency ultrasound, in which the first frequency is f 1 = f 0-Δf/2 and the second frequency is f 2 = f 0 + Δf/2. The frequency difference between the two components Δf is set as (a) Δf = 0.2 MHz, (b) Δf = 0.3 MHz and (c) Δf = 0.4 MHz.

Frequency-response curves of two-bubble systems with different ultrasound amplitude ratios between the two components of dual-frequency driving β, including (a) β = 0.1, (b) β = 1 and (c) β = 10. Note that f 0 = (f 1 + f 2)/2, where f 1 = f 0-Δf/2, f 2 = f 0 + Δf/2 and Δf = 0.3 MHz.

Effects of the acoustic pressure amplitude pA on the cavitation dynamics of a two-bubble system (R 01 = 3.2 μm and R 02 = 8.2 μm). (a) Oscillation amplitudes as a function of pA , as well as (b) and (c) the bifurcation diagrams of the normalized radius (R/R 0) of the two-bubble system as a function of pA for the non-interacting (w/o) and interacting (w) cases, respectively.

Oscillation amplitudes of a two-bubble system (i.e., Rmax
1/R 01 and Rmax
2/R 02) as a function of medium elasticity G and medium viscosity μ, including (a) a small bubble (R 01 = 3.2 μm) and (b) a large bubble (R 02 = 8.2 μm). (c) Bifurcation diagram of the normalized bubble radii (R/R 0) for the two bubbles as a function of G at a fixed μ = 1 mPa∙s. The symbol ‘w/o’ means that the interaction effects between bubbles are not considered. When the interaction effects are considered (w), the corresponding results are presented in (d)-(f), respectively.
Default parameter values for numerical simulations.

head.ce:title: Investigation of interaction effects on dual-frequency driven cavitation dynamics in a two-bubble system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The cavitation dynamics of a two-bubble system in viscoelastic media excited by dual-frequency ultrasound is studied numerically with a focus on the effects of inter-bubble interactions. Compared to the isolated bubble cases, the enhancement or suppression effects can be exerted on the amplitude and nonlinearity of the bubble oscillations to different degrees. Moreover, the interaction effects are found to be highly sensitive to multiple paramount parameters related to the two-bubble system, the dual-frequency ultrasound and the medium viscoelasticity. Specifically, the larger bubble of a two-bubble system shows a stronger effect on the smaller one, and this effect becomes more pronounced when the larger bubble undergoes harmonic and/or subharmonic resonances as well as the two bubbles get closer (e.g., d 0 < 100 μm). For the influences of the dual-frequency excitation, the results show that the bubbles can achieve enhanced harmonic and/or subharmonic oscillations as the frequency combinations with small frequency differences (e.g., Δf < 0.2 MHz) close to the corresponding resonance frequencies of bubbles, and the interaction effects are consequently intensified. Similarly, the bubble oscillations and the interaction effects can also be enhanced as the acoustic pressure amplitude of each frequency component is equal and the pressure amplitude pA increases. Above a pressure threshold (pA = 215 kPa), a larger bubble undergoes period 2 (P2) oscillations, which can force a smaller bubble to change its oscillation pattern from period 1 (P1) into P2 oscillations. In addition, it is found that the medium viscosity dampens the bubble oscillations while the medium elasticity affects the bubble resonances, accordingly exhibiting stronger interaction effects at smaller viscosities (e.g., μ < 4 mPa·s) or certain elasticities (approximately G = 70–120 kPa, G = 160–200 kPa and G = 640–780 kPa) at which the bubble resonances occur. The study can contribute to a better understanding of the complex dynamic behaviors of interacting cavitation bubbles in viscoelastic tissues for high efficient cavitation-mediated biomedical applications using dual-frequency ultrasound.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrasonic cavitation in biological soft tissues attracts much attention due to its importance for a wide range of biomedical applications, e.g., sonothrombolysis [1,2], shockwave lithotripsy [3], high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) ablation [4–6], and transient opening of biological barriers to promote drug delivery [7–9], etc. There is general evidence that the cavitation bubble dynamics and the cavitation-associated effects are highly responsible for these applications, and enhancing cavitation activity appropriately can result in high-efficient outcomes under certain conditions. Compared to the commonly used single-frequency ultrasound, ample evidence indicates that the dual-frequency ultrasound can result in stronger cavitation activity, thus significantly enhancing cavitation effects at an equivalent level of total acoustic power [10–13]. However, the introduction of an additional ultrasound excitation increases the complexity of cavitation bubble dynamics due to the synergistic effects of dual-frequency operation and the added influencing factors. Hence, it is desirable to understand the complex cavitation dynamics excited by dual-frequency ultrasound in biological soft tissues for achieving parameter optimization and further facilitating the enhanced ultrasonic cavitation to high-efficient biomedical applications.

Plenty of experimental work has established that dual-frequency excitation can noticeably enhance the cavitation activity [14,15], the thrombolysis efficiency [2,16,17], the therapeutic outcome of HIFU ablation [10,18,19], and so on. Since the cavitation bubble is on the µm scale in size and its oscillation period is on the order of µs, as well as almost all biological tissues are nearly opaque, the direct experimental visualization of the complex bubble dynamics in biological tissues during the dual-frequency ultrasonic cavitation remains a great challenge. In an attempt to circumvent this limitation, some researchers have investigated the time-dependent cavitation bubble dynamics in water via numerical simulations at the micrometer and microsecond scales, demonstrating that dual-frequency excitation can result in the combination and simultaneous resonances of bubble oscillation [20], an enhancement in bubble collapse strength [12,21], a decrease in cavitation threshold [22] and a higher bubble shape stability [23]. Recently, a few numerical studies have been devoted to investigating the dynamics of a dual-frequency driven cavitation bubble that is embedded in viscoelastic soft materials [13,24,25]. Specifically, the governing equations for the single bubble dynamics (e.g., Keller-Miksis and Gilmore equations) were modified by introducing additional viscoelastic models (e.g., Kelvin–Voigt and Zener models) accounting for the medium viscoelasticity. Subsequently, these governing equations were solved numerically under the time-dependent dual-frequency acoustic driving signals to obtain the bubble radius with respect to time. It was reported that the single bubble dynamics is influenced by various factors, such as the initial bubble size, the settings of dual-frequency ultrasound (e.g., the component frequency/amplitude combinations, the acoustic pressure amplitude, etc.) and the properties of the surrounding medium (i.e., viscosity, elasticity, etc.) [13,24,25]. Consequently, the inertial cavitation threshold [24,25] and the mechanical effects of cavitation [13] can also be essentially affected, potentially exhibiting either an enhanced or a weakened effect under the dual-frequency driving relative to the single-frequency case. Therefore, it is of great importance and urgent necessity to optimize the key parameters affecting the cavitation bubble dynamics for achieving the best performance of dual-frequency driving.

In contrast to the single bubble dynamics, the bubble dynamics affected by the neighboring bubbles differs considerably owing to the inter-bubble interactions, which has been well established by reliable evidence from a number of experimental and numerical studies [26–31]. The neighboring bubbles can alter the acoustic pressure experienced by the observed bubble through their radiated pressure waves and vice versa. Under single-frequency driving, it has been conclusively shown that the bubble oscillations can be enhanced or suppressed by the inter-bubble interactions, depending on the acoustic settings, the initial bubble sizes, the inter-bubble distances and the medium viscoelasticity [26–31]. For the dual-frequency case, the secondary Bjerknes force between two gas bubbles in liquids has been examined to reveal the mutual interactions between bubbles [32]. It was found that the sign and the strength of the Bjerknes force can be divided into nine regions while there are only four regions in the single-frequency case, indicating that the inter-bubble interactions under dual-frequency excitation become more complex. When acoustic cavitation occurs in viscoelastic media, the effects of medium viscoelasticity also need to be considered, consequently making the inter-bubble interactions even more complicated. Thus, it is crucial to reveal the complex inter-bubble interactions and how they affect the cavitation dynamics in viscoelastic tissues under dual-frequency driving. However, to date, there is little published work specifically addressing these issues.

The present study numerically investigates the dynamics of a two-bubble system in the viscoelastic medium with nonlinear elasticity under dual-frequency excitation, focusing on the effects of inter-bubble interactions on both the amplitude and nonlinearity of the bubble oscillations. The Keller-Miksis equation is coupled with the Kelvin–Voigt viscoelastic constitutive relation with nonlinear elasticity to simulate the bubble dynamics. The paper is organized as follows. In the following Sec. 2, the governing equations for cavitation dynamics of two interacting bubbles in a neo-Hookean medium are first derived, and then the analysis method for nonlinear bubble oscillations under dual-frequency driving is described in detail. In Sec. 3, the interaction effects on the cavitation bubble dynamics at some paramount parameters (i.e., parameters related to the two-bubble system, the dual-frequency ultrasound and the medium viscoelasticity) are illustrated. Section 4 concludes the main findings of the present work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modelling two bubble dynamics under dual-frequency driving
ce:section.ce:para:
The Keller-Miksis (KM) equation is used to investigate the cavitation dynamics of a two-bubble system with considering the interactions between bubbles via adding the influence of acoustic waves radiated by the other bubble. The modified KM equation for bubble i is [33,34]

are the time-dependent radii of bubbles i and j, respectively. The dot stands for the derivative with respect to time t. c is the sound speed in the surrounding medium, ρ is the medium density, and σ is the surface tension at the bubble wall. d 0 is the center-to-center distance between the two bubbles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modelling viscoelastic effects of surrounding media on bubble dynamics
ce:section.ce:para:
A Kelvin–Voigt-based constitutive model with non-linear elasticity is used to relate the deviatoric stress and strain tensors in the surrounding medium during bubble oscillations. The stress is the sum of viscous and elastic contributions [36–38],
(5)

Hence, coupling the Eqs. (1)-(3) and (10) can obtain the governing equations for the cavitation dynamics of two interacting bubbles under dual-frequency excitation which are embedded in a viscoelastic medium with nonlinear neo-Hookean elasticity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bifurcation analysis method with Poincaré section of dual-frequency driving
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the dynamic behaviors of a two-bubble system within a wide range of control parameters, bifurcation analysis is performed to clarify the nonlinear responses of bubbles to the dual-frequency driving. Specifically, Poincaré sections are taken at each driving period to generate the bifurcation diagram by sampling the Ri (t) curves at time instances
τ n
= n ∙ T
(
n
=
0 ,
1 ,
2 , . . .
), where n and T are the period number and the period of dual-frequency ultrasound, respectively. It can be summarized as [39–41]
(11)
P ≡
R i
Θ
R i
t
,
R ̇
i
t
: Θ =
τ n
where P denotes the points in the bifurcation diagram. For the dual-frequency driving in this study, it is periodic due to the ratio of each frequency pair is always rational, and its period T can be calculated by [42]
(12)
T
=
L C M ( 1 /
f 1
, 1 /
f 2
)
where
LCM
denotes the least common multiple of the periods of the two frequency components (i.e.,
T 1
= 1 /
f 1
and
T 2
= 1 /
f 2
, respectively). Note that the bifurcation diagrams of the normalized bubble oscillations (
R i
/
R
0 i
) are plotted at
τ
n
=
100 T ∼
150 T for a steady-state solution.

In our simulations, the bubble size is set to be much smaller than the wavelength of driving acoustic wave and the inter-bubble distance is always large enough, yielding the assumption that the cavitation bubbles are spherical in shape during their oscillations [30,43,44]. Unless otherwise indicated, the simulation parameters are set as shown in Table 1 . Note that the default values of driving frequencies (f 1 and f 2) are set to 1.1 MHz and 1.3 MHz according to the previous study [24], which are within the − 6 dB bandwidth of a commercial HIFU transducer (H102, Sonic Concepts, Woodinville, WA, USA). The coupled second-order ordinary differential equations (i.e., Eq. (1) for i = 1, 2 and j = 2-i) are solved numerically using the Runge-Kutta method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction effects of two bubbles under dual-frequency driving
Representative interaction effects on cavitation dynamics
ce:section.ce:para:
Under dual-frequency ultrasonic excitation, there are some representative interaction effects acting on the amplitude and nonlinearity of bubble oscillations, as illustrated in Figs. 1 and 2
. In the bifurcation analysis for the nonlinear bubble oscillations, the color-coded cycles represent the values of normalized bubble oscillations (R 1/R 01) after every period of dual-frequency driving (i.e., n = 100, 101,…105) for both non-interacting (Fig. 1(a)) and interacting (Fig. 1(b)-(e)) cases. There exists only one value for all red circles in the non-interacting case, therefore the bubble undergoes Period-1 (P1) oscillations, which means the bubble oscillation is exactly periodic with the driving force. Comparing Fig. 1(a) and (b), it can be seen that the oscillation amplitude of bubble 1 (R 01 = 2.5 μm) can be slightly enlarged (R 1max/R 01 = 1.722 for the isolated bubble case vs. R 1max/R 01 = 1.769 for the case considering the interactions with a nearby bubble having an initial radius of R 02 = 3.2 μm). In contrast, a suppression effect is observed (R 1max/R 01 = 1.645) when the bubble is affected by an adjacent bubble of R 02 = 7 μm, but there are two values at each driving period (Fig. 1(c)), indicating that the oscillation of bubble 1 repeats itself after two forcing periods and it is called Period-2 (P2) oscillations. Similarly, the P2 oscillations can also be manifested in Fig. 1(d) but the bubble oscillation amplitude is enlarged (R 1max/R 01 = 1.789) for the case of R 02 = 8.2 μm. As the R 02 increases to 10 μm, the oscillation amplitude of the bubble 1 can be obviously suppressed, and its oscillations convert back into the P1 oscillations, as presented in Fig. 1(e). Note that the beneficial interaction effect on facilitating the occurrence of P2 oscillations in certain cases can contribute to generating sub-harmonic emissions, which is especially useful for contrast-enhanced ultrasound imaging applications [45,46] and/or cavitation monitoring with these special acoustic emissions [47,48].

In addition to the enhancement effect on the occurrence of P2 oscillations, the P2 oscillations can also be suppressed due to the interaction effects, hence exhibiting a transition into P1 oscillations, as exemplified in Fig. 2. It can be obvious in Fig. 2(a) that an isolated bubble with R 01 = 8.2 μm undergoes P2 oscillations and the oscillation amplitude is R 1max/R 01 = 1.547. The P2 oscillations can be slightly enhanced owing to a minor increase in the oscillation amplitude (R 1max/R 01 = 1.560 seen in Fig. 2(b)) as the bubble interacts with a small bubble (R 02 = 3.2 μm). By contrast, the amplitude of the P2 oscillations can also be reduced due to the interactions with a neighboring bubble of R 02 = 7 μm (Fig. 2(c)). Moreover, it is worth pointing out that a transition from P2 to P1 oscillations and a distinct decrease in the oscillation amplitude can be induced by the inter-bubble interactions with an adjacent larger bubble (R 02 = 10 μm), as presented in Fig. 2(d).

Overall, these results suggest that the interactions between adjacent bubbles are considerably complex, affecting the bubble dynamics with different effects (i.e., suppression/enlargement effects on the oscillation amplitude and the oscillation pattern) to varying degrees. Moreover, under single-frequency excitation, it has demonstrated that the bubble dynamics are strongly affected by the initial bubble radii, the inter-bubble distances, the ultrasound parameters and the medium viscoelasticity [26–31]. Thus, the effects of these paramount parameters are examined in the following sections for the dual-frequency case. Furthermore, a better and deeper understanding into the complex inter-bubble interactions and their effective influences on the bubble dynamics in viscoelastic media is urgently needed, potentially allowing us to further optimize the dynamic behaviors of interacting bubbles in the desired dual-frequency applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the parameters related to the two-bubble system

ce:section.ce:para: As exemplified in Figs. 1 and 2, the initial bubble radii are the crucial factor in determining the interaction effects between bubbles. Firstly, differently-sized bubbles will respond differently to the same ultrasound driving, such as a small bubble undergoing the P1 oscillations shown in Fig. 1(a) while a large bubble exhibiting P2 oscillations displayed in Fig. 2(a). Secondly, for a two-bubble system, the interaction effects between bubbles with different initial radii will also be considerably different. More specifically, either an enlargement or a suppression effect can be exerted on the bubble, greatly affecting its oscillation amplitude and oscillation pattern. The effects of the initial bubble radii are investigated in detail as shown in Fig. 3 .

The oscillation amplitudes (R max/R 0) of a two-bubble system (R 01 = 2.5 μm) as a function of the initial radius of the second bubble (R 02 = 1–10 μm) are presented in Fig. 3(a). When the interaction effects are not considered, as the R 02 increases, the oscillation amplitude of the isolated bubble 1 (R 1-w/o) remains unchanged, whereas there is an evident variation in the oscillation amplitude of the isolated bubble 2 (R 2-w/o). Note that a main peak is formed near R 02 = 3.2 μm, where the bubble 2 is driven on-resonance and its oscillation amplitude is maximal. As the interaction effects are taken into account, it can be seen that the oscillation amplitude of the bubble 1 (R 1-w) changes dramatically with the R 02 increasing, which is prominently enlarged when the bubble 2 oscillates at the on-resonance conditions while it is notably suppressed as the R 02 is sufficiently large (approximately R 02 > 4 μm). It should also be noted that the oscillation amplitude of the bubble 1 can be enlarged when the large bubble 2 undergoes the sub-resonances (marked by arrows). In comparison, the oscillation amplitude of the bubble 2 (R 2-w) seems to be mostly unaffected except for the cases where the oscillation amplitude is slightly reduced as the R 02 is small (approximately R 02 < 2 μm).

The bifurcation diagrams of the bubble 1 and bubble 2 without (w/o) or with (w) the interaction effects are also depicted with respect to the R 02 as shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c), respectively. For the isolated bubbles, the bubble 1 remains P1 oscillations, while the bubble 2 undergoes a transition from P1 to P2 oscillations through a period doubling (PD) at R 02 = 6.7 μm and R 02 = 8.1 μm, as highlighted by the red arrows in Fig. 3(c). As a result of the interactions between bubbles, it is apparent that the bubble 1 has 3 different oscillation regions, i.e., the P1 oscillations (blue arrows), the initiation of the PD (red arrows) and the P2 oscillations (green arrows). This evidence indicates that one large bubble (R 02 = 6.7∼7.1 μm or R 02 = 8.1∼ 8.6 μm) that undergoes P2 oscillations can force one smaller bubble (R 01 = 2.5 μm) to undergo PD and consequently the oscillation pattern can be forced from P1 into P2 oscillations. Similarly, the corresponding results for a large bubble 1 (R 01 = 8.2 μm) are presented in Fig. 3(d)-(f), respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 3(d) that the oscillations of the bubble 1 are distinctly suppressed by the interactions with the bubble 2 as it is large (approximately R 02 > 3.8 μm). Conversely, the inter-bubble interactions affect the bubble 2 in the opposite way, i.e., when the bubble 2 is small (about R 02 < 4.5 μm), an obvious suppression effect exerts on the oscillations of the bubble 2. From the perspective of the oscillation pattern, Fig. 3(e) displays that the isolated bubble 1 initially undergoes P2 oscillations, which can be forced into P1 oscillations when it interacts with the neighboring large bubbles (approximately R 02 > 8 μm). Due to the inter-bubble interactions, the bubble 1 undergoing P2 oscillations can also force smaller bubbles (e.g., R 02 < 6.7 μm) to change their oscillation patterns from P1 into P2 oscillations, as shown in Fig. 3(f). These forced oscillations are consistent with previous findings under single-frequency excitation [27,49], and the results contribute additional evidence that the forced P2 oscillations can also take place for the dual-frequency driving case.

Taken together, these results evidently suggest that the amplitude and pattern of bubble oscillations strongly depend on not only the initial bubble radius but also the interactions between bubbles. Furthermore, it also indicates that the large bubble of a two-bubble system plays a dominant role in the interaction effects rather than the small one. Considering that the inter-bubble interactions mean the influences of acoustic waves radiated by the other bubbles, the radiated acoustic pressure of each bubble is positively proportional to its instantaneous radius as indicated by the Eq. (1). Thus, it is expected that the radiated pressures from a small bubble result in weaker pressure fields that do not significantly perturb the dynamics of a large bubble.

As a powerful factor influencing the bubble–bubble interactions [29,30], the inter-bubble distance d 0 severely affects the cavitation dynamics of the two interacting bubbles. Fig. 4 shows the dependence of the cavitation bubble dynamics on the d 0 in a two-bubble system with dual-frequency excitation. It is evident that the farther the two bubbles are apart from each other, the less the differences between the non-interacting and interacting cases become. In particular, the single bubble case can be regarded as an exception of two interacting bubbles with an infinite distance between them. Moreover, comparing the results shown in Fig. 4(a) and (b), it is also demonstrated that the interaction effects tend to dramatically affect the smaller bubble of the two-bubble system, for instance, exhibiting strong effects on the bubble 1 (R 01 = 2.5 μm) as R 02 is larger than about 2.5 μm (Fig. 4(a)) and on the bubble 2 as R 02 < 2 μm (as highlighted by the dashed box in Fig. 4(b)). For further investigation of this phenomenon, the oscillation amplitudes of a two-bubble system (R 01 = 2.5 μm and R 02 = 8.2 μm) are also plotted as a function of d 0 in Fig. 4(c), and the results display a clear decreasing trend in the differences between the non-interacting and interacting cases with d 0 increasing. In addition, Fig. 4(d) illustrates the inter-bubble distance dependent bifurcation diagram of the two-bubble system. It can be found that at smaller inter-bubble distances, the smaller bubble 1 can be forced to undergo the PD with a transition from P1 to P2 oscillations as a result of the interactions with the larger bubble 2 which undergoes P2 oscillations. By contrast, the bubble 2 maintains its P2 oscillations within the whole distances examined here, whereas the bubble 1 gets back to its P1 oscillations (marked by the black arrow) when the two bubbles are sufficiently far apart from each other probably due to the negligible interaction effects. Taken together, these results elucidate how the inter-bubble distance affects the oscillation amplitude and the oscillation pattern of two interacting bubbles, giving a nonlinear dependence on the inter-bubble distance. Specifically, the interaction effect becomes much stronger as the two bubbles get closer, while it is negligible at sufficiently large distances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of acoustic settings with dual-frequency excitation
ce:section.ce:para: In this sub-section, the influences of some key parameters related to the dual-frequency ultrasound on the cavitation dynamics of the two-bubble system are numerically investigated. For the two-bubble system, the initial radii of the bubble 1 and bubble 2 are set as R 01 = 3.2 μm and R 02 = 8.2 μm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combinations of the two component frequencies
ce:section.ce:para: Under dual-frequency excitation, there are two ultrasound driving signals and the cavitation dynamics of two-bubble systems with setting different frequency combinations of the two components are first examined (Fig. 5 ). Specifically, the bubble oscillation amplitudes are plotted as a function of the frequency f 0 = (f 1 + f 2)/2, where the first frequency is f 1 = f 0-Δf/2 and the second frequency is f 2 = f 0 + Δf/2. The frequency difference between the two components Δf is also changed to illustrate the dependence of the bubble response on the frequency difference.

It can be found in Fig. 5(a) that the frequency–response curves for the bubble 1 (R 1) only show one single resonance peak (marked by the black arrow), as both resonance peaks corresponding to either f 1 or f 2 are too close to be distinguished due to a small frequency difference Δf = 0.2 MHz. With an increase in Δf, two separate resonance peaks can be observed as highlighted by the black arrows, as shown in Fig. 5(b) and (c). Furthermore, it is also important to point out that the oscillation amplitude can be enlarged as the Δf decreases, suggesting that the bubble response can be enhanced via the merging of the resonance regions. This finding also indicates that the closer the frequencies of the two components get, the stronger the bubble oscillations become.

For the larger bubble 2, the main resonance peak is generated at a much smaller frequency, probably due to the inverse dependence of resonance frequency on the bubble size [50,51]. Moreover, subharmonic resonances of the bubble 2 are also obtained as marked by the red arrows, which are consistent with the results shown in Fig. 1-4. Under the simulation conditions examined here, it seems that the larger bubble 2 is more likely to undergo subharmonic oscillations rather than the smaller bubble 1. In addition, by comparing the results without (w/o) and with (w) taking into account the interaction effects, another important observation is that the smaller bubble 1 can undergo enhanced oscillations when the oscillations of the larger bubble 2 becomes on-resonance. This can be ascribed to the constructive interactions between bubbles as demonstrated under single-frequency excitation [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasound amplitude ratio between the two components
ce:section.ce:para: Further investigation into the dynamics of a two-bubble system is also performed at different ultrasound amplitude ratios between the two components of dual-frequency ultrasound β. Increasing β means a gradual energy shift from the low-frequency component to the high-frequency one. As shown in Fig. 6 , for both cases without and with the bubble–bubble interactions, there is only one resonance peak in the frequency–response curve for the bubble 1 as β = 0.1 or β = 10. In comparison, there are two resonance peaks for the case of β = 1 (Fig. 6(b)). It can be explained that the acoustic energy is mainly concentrated on either one of the two components when the β is much larger or smaller than 1, resulting in one resonance peak corresponding to the frequency component with much higher energy. Moreover, it can be seen that the resonance peaks shift to a lower frequency with β increasing. Similarly, for the bubble 2, two subharmonic resonance peaks are formed as β = 1. These results highlight that as the acoustic pressure amplitudes of the two frequency components are sufficiently close or equal to each other, dual-frequency excitation can maintain the characteristic responses of the bubbles to each frequency component.

The influences of the acoustic pressure amplitude pA on the cavitation dynamics of a two-bubble system (R 01 = 3.2 μm and R 02 = 8.2 μm) are shown in Fig. 7 . It can be clearly seen in Fig. 7(a) that the oscillation amplitude of the bubble 1 is remarkably reduced by the bubble–bubble interactions, and the larger the pA is, the more pronounced the interaction effects become. This could reasonably be expected since the oscillation of the neighboring bubble can be notably intensified at a larger pA . Accordingly, the interactions between bubbles increase with the pA increasing, which has also been well demonstrated in previous studies under single-frequency excitation [29,52,53]. Moreover, as exemplified in the bifurcation analysis of the normalized radii (R/R 0) of each isolated bubble as a function of pA , there is an obvious enhancement in the nonlinearity of bubble oscillations with an increase of pA (Fig. 7(b). More specifically, it is evident that both bubbles undergo P1 oscillations as pA is small, then with pA increasing, only the oscillations of the large bubble 2 undergo period doubling at a pressure threshold (pA = 215 kPa) above which the oscillations become P2 pattern. A possible explanation for this might be that the occurrence of P2 oscillations is size dependent, as demonstrated by the bifurcation diagram of the R/R 0 for the bubble 2 with respect to its initial radius R 02 shown in Fig. 3(c). Additionally, as displayed in Fig. 7(c), the dynamics of interacting bubbles at a low pA are similar to the non-interacting case, both bubbles undergo P1 oscillations. In contrast, increasing pA above the same pressure threshold of 215 kPa as that of the isolated bubble 2 can lead to the generation of P2 oscillations for both bubbles. These results further confirm previous findings that a small bubble can be forced by a larger bubble to exhibit P2 oscillations, as shown in Figs. 1-4.

Fig. 8 examines how the cavitation dynamics of a two-bubble system and the interaction effects depend on the viscoelastic parameters (i.e., medium viscosity μ and medium elasticity G) under dual-frequency acoustic driving. When the inter-bubble interactions are not considered, it is obvious that increasing the medium viscosity results in a noticeable decrease in the oscillation amplitude for either the bubble 1 (Rmax
1/R 01-w/o) or the bubble 2 (Rmax
2/R 02-w/o) as shown in Fig. 8(a) and (b), respectively. In comparison, it seems to affect the smaller bubble 1 to a greater degree than the larger bubble 2, particularly for the cases at smaller medium elasticities. As for the effects of medium elasticity, it can be seen in Fig. 8(b) that there are three obvious peaks in the variations of bubble oscillation amplitude as a function of medium elasticity at sufficiently small medium viscosities (μ < 4 mPa∙s), due to the bubble resonances at certain medium elasticities [37,49]. More specifically, the resonant peaks at smaller medium elasticities (approximately G = 70–120 kPa and G = 160–200 kPa, marked by the white arrows) are the subharmonic resonances, while the resonant peak at larger medium elasticities (approximately G = 640–780 kPa, marked by the black arrow) is the main harmonic resonance, which is supported by the bifurcation analysis of the bubble oscillations as a function of G (Fig. 8(c)) at μ = 1 mPa∙s. It is evident that only the bubble 2 undergoes P2 oscillations at the medium elasticities where subharmonic resonances occur as highlighted by the gray shadows in Fig. 8(c).

Moreover, when the interactions between the two bubbles are considered, the corresponding results are presented in Fig. 8(d)-(f), respectively. It can be found in Fig. 8(d) that the oscillation amplitude of the bubble 1 is remarkably suppressed as both viscosity and elasticity are sufficiently small, whereas its oscillation amplitude is greatly enlarged when the bubble 2 undergoes resonant oscillations. On the contrary, the oscillation amplitude of the bubble 2 seems to be almost unaffected with a comparison of results shown in Fig. 8(b) and (e). Additionally, at a small medium viscosity (μ = 1 mPa∙s), the bubble 1 is also forced into P2 oscillations owing to the interaction effects from the bubble 2 as displayed in Fig. 8(f). Overall, these results indicate that both the viscosity and elasticity of the surrounding medium can considerably affect the amplitude and pattern of bubble oscillations. Specifically, medium viscosity mainly dampens the bubble oscillations, while medium elasticity trends to affect the bubble resonances. Furthermore, another important finding is that the interaction effects on the dynamic behaviors of the two-bubble system seem to be negligible when the values of the viscosity and/or elasticity are sufficiently large. Nevertheless, it is worth pointing out that for a two-bubble system with different sizes examined here, the interaction effects on the smaller bubble should be seriously considered when the medium viscosity is small and/or the larger bubble is driven on-resonance at certain medium elasticities.

This study has examined the effects of inter-bubble interactions on the amplitude and nonlinearity of bubble oscillations in a two-bubble system that is embedded in viscoelastic media and driven by dual-frequency ultrasound. It is found that the inter-bubble interactions can exert the enhancement or suppression effects on the amplitude and nonlinearity of the bubble oscillations to different degrees, which are highly sensitive to multiple paramount parameters related to the two-bubble system, the dual-frequency driving signal and the medium viscoelasticity. The important findings of this study can be summarized as follows:
1)
Differently-sized bubbles will respond differently to the same ultrasound driving and the oscillations of the larger bubble can dramatically affect that of the smaller one, in particular for certain cases where the larger bubble undergoes harmonic and/or subharmonic resonances. Moreover, the interaction effects gradually decrease and even can be negligible with the inter-bubble distance increasing.
2)
Small frequency differences in the frequency combinations of dual-frequency ultrasound close to the harmonic and/or subharmonic resonance frequencies of the bubbles achieve the best performance, enhancing the bubble oscillation amplitude and/or subharmonic oscillations, as well as the interaction effects. Similarly, the bubble oscillations and the interaction effects can also be amplified as the pressure amplitude of each component is equal and the pressure amplitude increases.
3)
Medium viscosity mainly dampens the bubble oscillations while the medium elasticity affects the bubble resonances, accordingly exhibiting stronger interaction effects at lower viscosities (e.g., μ < 4 mPa·s) or certain elasticities (approximately G = 70–120 kPa, G = 160–200 kPa and G = 640–780 kPa) where the bubble resonances occur.

The present study may make several noteworthy contributions to further understandings the interaction behaviors of bubbles in viscoelastic media under dual-frequency excitation, as well as the resultant effects on the bubble oscillation amplitude and the nonlinearity of bubble dynamics, which are greatly valuable for a wide range of dual-frequency applications. Notably, it is worth mentioning that the used Keller-Miksis equation for simulating bubble dynamics can be applied for a wide range of Mach numbers
R ̇
/ c < 1
[54], nevertheless, the Mach number may exceed this limit at sufficiently high acoustic pressures and the Gilmore-Akulichev model should be used owing to its large applicability range extending to
R ̇
/ c < 2.2
[54]. More research using the Gilmore-Akulichev model is needed to assess the cavitation bubble dynamics more accurately, providing a superior choice for a wide range of applications. Additionally, since this paper mainly focused on the interaction effects between two dual-frequency driven bubbles, for simplicity, the effects of heat and mass transfer across the bubble were neglected. For more accurate simulations of the bubble dynamics, these effects also need be considered in future studies.

Dui Qin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Qianru Yang: Methodology, Software, Validation. Shuang Lei: Software, Validation. Jia Fu: Visualization. Xiaojuan Ji: Conceptualization, Resources. Xiuxin Wang: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11904042), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022MD723728) and the Natural Science Foundation Project of Chongqing (CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0861)
